interactions.summary: Jiang completes the black/white symbolic inversion, then moves into the most controversial difference: women's bodily and sexual agency versus modern marital ownership and sexual taboos.
He gives biological and primate examples for female sexual agency, including rhesus monkeys, bonobos, female-female sex as unity, and cultures where females initiate sex.

He argues that gender hierarchy and racial color symbolism are cultural constructions: plow agriculture lowered female participation, and white-good/black-bad came from race ideology used to justify European conquest.

He uses Sex at Dawn, human anatomy, polyandry, Mother Goddess religion, and Melanesian semen-energy beliefs to argue that for much of human history sex and child care were organized around community rather than genetic ownership.

speaker_notes.note: The packet is continuous Jiang lecture with classroom prompts. No independent student question is recoverable inside the focus refs.

claims.claim: Jiang says modern marital norms treat a woman's body as belonging to her husband, whereas for most of human history a woman's body was understood as her own.
Sexual taboos and the stud/slut double standard are presented as cultural tools for shaming women into proper behavior and controlling women's bodies.
claims.temporal_scope: Jiang's diagnosis of modern patriarchal norms

claims.claim: Jiang argues that female sexual agency is not biologically abnormal, citing female-initiated sex in primates, bonobo social sex, and cultures where females initiate sex with males.

Plow agriculture is presented as a turning point: once men's labor contributed more materially, societies showed lower female participation in politics and labor, making gender hierarchy cultural rather than biological.
Jiang says the idea that white is good and black is bad is a recent racial construct developed to justify European imperial conquest.
Against the modern gene-first model, Jiang says Mother Goddess religion made all children communal responsibilities because all children belonged to the same sacred family.
Jiang describes a belief system in which women give life and men give energy; under that logic, multiple male partners can increase the woman's or child's energy rather than threaten ownership.
claims.temporal_scope: Example from Melanesian beliefs as presented in the lecture

signature_moments.moment: Modern marriage is exposed as a property claim over the woman's body.
signature_moments.source_phrase: we assume the woman's body belongs to the husband
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It connects sex, patriarchy, and property before the lecture makes that link explicit near the end.

signature_moments.moment: The stud/slut double standard becomes the mechanism by which patriarchy disciplines women.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the best way to do that is by controlling the woman's body
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It preserves the lecture's blunt account of shame as a political technology.

signature_moments.moment: Before the individual, there is only the community: my child and my friend's child are both children of the Mother Goddess.
signature_moments.source_phrase: There was no concept of individual. There was only a concept of community.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This is the lecture's deepest explanation for why sexual agency does not have to produce social chaos.

signature_moments.moment: The maternal body gives life; male bodies supply energy, so more men can mean more energy rather than more shame.
signature_moments.source_phrase: the woman, she gives life. The man gives energy.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The phrase carries the alien logic Jiang wants students to inhabit before judging old sexual customs.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: The capacity of women to choose sexual partners and initiate sex, contrasted with patriarchal ownership of women's bodies.
Rules and shame codes Jiang says discipline women into patriarchal standards of proper behavior.
A social meaning treated as natural even though Jiang says it is historically produced, such as gender hierarchy, race, or black/white moral symbolism.
The modern assumption that people primarily organize mating and child care to spread and protect their own genes.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang's 'recent construct' claim about race and black/white symbolism should remain dated to this lecture and checked before promotion, especially because the transcript says '20 years ago.'

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR says 'Reese's monkeys'; context suggests rhesus monkeys, but the transcript was not rewritten.
The phrase 'race was invented about 20 years ago' is likely a speech or ASR anomaly; do not use the exact date without checking the source.
